name: Psilocybin Mushrooms
description: Psilocybin mushrooms, known by various indigenous names such as 'teonanácatl' (divine flesh) among the Nahuatl-speaking peoples, held a profound spiritual significance in Mesoamerican cultures, particularly among the Aztec, Maya, and other groups. These mushrooms were not merely hallucinogens but rather sacred entities believed to facilitate communication with the divine, ancestors, and other realms. Their use was deeply embedded in religious rituals and healing practices, viewed as a means to gain insight, prophecy, and spiritual healing. The consumption of psilocybin mushrooms was often conducted under the guidance of a shaman or healer, within a controlled and ceremonial setting. They played a pivotal role in divination, diagnosing illnesses, and seeking solutions to community problems. The imagery associated with these mushrooms, including mushroom stones and depictions in codices, provides compelling evidence of their importance. The arrival of the Spanish conquistadors led to the suppression of these practices, deeming them pagan and idolatrous, yet the indigenous communities continued to preserve their knowledge and traditions in secret, ensuring the survival of this ancient spiritual practice. The mushrooms were seen as a bridge between the physical and spiritual worlds, allowing individuals to transcend ordinary consciousness and access deeper understanding.
subtitle: Divine Flesh: A Sacred Mesoamerican Herb
preparations: Applied topically in some rare instances

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Teonanácatl
keyMyths.description: According to some indigenous accounts, Teonanácatl originated from the blood of deities or sacred figures, solidifying its divine status. This origin story links the mushroom to the very essence of the cosmos and the power of creation.

keyMyths.title: Mushroom Stones and Divine Rulers
keyMyths.description: Mushroom stones, found in archaeological sites, depict rulers and deities associated with mushrooms, suggesting the use of psilocybin in royal and elite circles. These stones served as both ritual objects and visual representations of the power derived from mushroom use.
keyMyths.source: Archaeological records and art historical analysis

sources.author: R. Gordon Wasson
